network.overall_strategy: A convergent prebiotic synthesis network for glycine featuring two major terminal routes (Strecker hydrolysis via aminoacetonitrile and reductive amination of glyoxylic acid) fed by multiple hydrothermal carbon-fixation front-ends (CO₂ reduction to formate, RWGS to CO, Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis) and surface-level photochemistry/condensation. Formaldehyde serves as the principal hub molecule, produced by two independent hydrothermal routes and consumed by both the Strecker branch and the formose-photooxidation branch. All reactions specify mineral catalysts. Environmental flow is strictly Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical with no backward transitions. A direct hydrothermal carbonylation-amination route (Huber & Wächtershäuser type) provides an additional short pathway. HCN photolysis to formamide and subsequent hydrolysis supplies supplementary NH₃ to surface-level Strecker and biochemical reductive amination reactions.

reactions.name: CO₂ hydrogenation to formate on greigite

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11, H₂-rich serpentinization fluid

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ adsorbs onto greigite surface Fe sites and undergoes two-electron reduction by H₂ chemisorbed on adjacent sulfide sites, forming surface-bound formate (HCOO⁻) which desorbs into alkaline vent fluid. Greigite acts as a structural and electronic analog of biological [Fe₄S₄] ferredoxin clusters.
reactions.reasoning: Experimentally demonstrated by Roldan et al. (2015) who showed CO₂ reduction to formate on greigite nanoparticles under hydrothermal conditions. Supported by Preiner et al. (2020) showing native iron and iron sulfide minerals catalyze CO₂ fixation to formate at 100°C under moderate H₂ pressures. This is the most thermodynamically favorable first step in abiotic carbon fixation at alkaline vents (Sojo et al., 2016).

reactions.name: Reverse water-gas shift reaction on magnetite

conditions.other: black smoker or high-temperature vent conditions

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ + H₂ → CO + H₂O on magnetite surface. Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ redox cycling at magnetite surface mediates oxygen transfer from CO₂ to H₂. The reaction is thermodynamically favored above ~450K. CO is a key C1 intermediate for subsequent Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: The reverse water-gas shift (RWGS) is well-established in heterogeneous catalysis and has been demonstrated on magnetite under simulated hydrothermal conditions by McCollom & Seewald (2006). CO production from CO₂ + H₂ on iron oxide minerals at 400-500K has been confirmed by He et al. (2019). This provides the CO feedstock needed for FTT synthesis.

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on native iron

conditions.other: strongly reducing conditions, awaruite-bearing serpentinization assemblage

reactions.mechanism: Formate adsorbs on metallic iron surface via oxygen coordination. Fe⁰ donates two electrons, reducing the carbonyl of formate to generate formaldehyde. H₂ regenerates the iron surface. FeS co-catalyst stabilizes the transition state through sulfide-mediated electron transfer. The thermodynamic barrier is overcome by the strongly reducing environment and continuous H₂ supply from serpentinization.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) demonstrated that native iron (awaruite) under serpentinization-like conditions reduces CO₂ through formate to formaldehyde and further reduced C1 species at 100°C. While formaldehyde is thermodynamically unstable and reactive, its steady-state concentration is maintained by continuous production. Herschy et al. (2014) showed that alkaline vent conditions stabilize formaldehyde via rapid consumption in downstream reactions. This is the rate-limiting step in the formate→formaldehyde conversion.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type formaldehyde synthesis on magnetite
reactions.mechanism: CO chemisorbs on magnetite surface Fe sites, undergoes partial hydrogenation to a surface formyl (HCO*) intermediate, then further hydrogenation yields formaldehyde (H₂CO) which desorbs. This is the first partial hydrogenation step in the Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) reaction series.
reactions.reasoning: FTT synthesis of oxygenated C1-C3 compounds including formaldehyde from CO + H₂ on magnetite and other iron oxide surfaces has been demonstrated by McCollom et al. (1999) and Rushdi & Simoneit (2001) under hydrothermal conditions. Holm & Neubeck (2009) showed that serpentinization-derived H₂ and CO in the presence of magnetite can produce formaldehyde and other small organics at 200-300°C. This provides an independent route to formaldehyde complementing the formate reduction pathway.

reactions.name: UV photocatalytic hydration of HCN to formamide

conditions.other: UV irradiation (λ < 300 nm), shallow pools, no ozone shielding

reactions.mechanism: UV photons excite electron-hole pairs in TiO₂ semiconductor surface. Photogenerated hydroxyl radicals (OH•) attack adsorbed HCN at the carbon center, forming an aminocarbonyl radical intermediate that is quenched by a second H-atom transfer to yield formamide (HCONH₂). Montmorillonite concentrates HCN on its interlayer surfaces, enhancing local concentration.
reactions.reasoning: Saladino et al. (2012, 2013) demonstrated that UV irradiation of HCN solutions in the presence of TiO₂ mineral photocatalysts efficiently produces formamide. Botta et al. (2017) showed that TiO₂-catalyzed photochemistry of HCN in water produces formamide as a major product under simulated early Earth UV conditions. Formamide is a key prebiotic building block that can be further hydrolyzed to formate and ammonia (Barks et al., 2010).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of formamide to formate and ammonia on clay

conditions.other: warm evaporitic pools, slightly alkaline pH 8-9

reactions.mechanism: Water attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of formamide adsorbed on montmorillonite interlayer surfaces. The tetrahedral intermediate collapses to release NH₃ and formate (HCOO⁻). Clay mineral surface Lewis acid sites (Al³⁺) activate the carbonyl toward nucleophilic addition. The released NH₃ becomes available for Strecker and reductive amination reactions in the same surface environment.
reactions.reasoning: Formamide hydrolysis is thermodynamically favorable and kinetically enhanced on clay mineral surfaces (Saladino et al., 2004). The reaction has been studied as part of the formamide-based prebiotic scenario by Saladino et al. (2012). The NH₃ product is critical for amino acid synthesis pathways. Importantly, this reaction maintains forward environmental flow: HCN (atmospherically delivered to surface) → formamide → formate + NH₃, all within the Surface environment, with NH₃ feeding directly into downstream Strecker chemistry.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of aminoacetonitrile from formaldehyde

conditions.other: slightly alkaline pH 8-9, concentrated shallow pools

reactions.mechanism: Step 1: Formaldehyde (HCHO) condenses with NH₃ to form methyleneimine (H₂C=NH) with loss of H₂O, catalyzed by clay mineral surface. Step 2: HCN undergoes nucleophilic addition to the electrophilic carbon of the imine, forming aminoacetonitrile (H₂NCH₂CN). Montmorillonite concentrates the reactants in interlayer spaces and provides Lewis acid activation of the imine. This is the classic Strecker mechanism applied to the simplest aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is the best-established prebiotic route to α-amino acids, first demonstrated by Miller (1957) in the context of prebiotic chemistry. Specifically for glycine, the Strecker route HCHO + NH₃ + HCN → aminoacetonitrile has been demonstrated under mild aqueous conditions by Danger et al. (2012) and reviewed by Pascal et al. (2005). Montmorillonite clay catalysis of Strecker-type reactions has been demonstrated by Orgel (1998) and Ferris (2005). The reaction proceeds readily at ambient temperatures and is considered the most robust prebiotic pathway to glycine.

reactions.name: Partial hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycinamide

conditions.other: warm aqueous pools, pH 7-9, wet-dry cycling assists

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of water to the nitrile (C≡N) group of aminoacetonitrile, proceeding through a 1,1-aminohydroxy intermediate that tautomerizes to glycinamide (H₂NCH₂CONH₂). The clay surface Lewis acid sites (Al³⁺, Mg²⁺) coordinate the nitrile nitrogen, activating the carbon toward water attack. Partial hydrolysis stops at the amide because the amide C-N bond is kinetically stable under these mild conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to amides is well-established in prebiotic chemistry and is the first step in the two-stage hydrolysis of aminonitriles to amino acids. Danger et al. (2012) demonstrated that aminoacetonitrile hydrolysis proceeds through glycinamide as an isolable intermediate under mild aqueous conditions. Montmorillonite catalysis of nitrile hydrolysis has been shown by Ferris et al. (2005). This partial hydrolysis is kinetically faster than the subsequent amide hydrolysis step.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of glycinamide to glycine

conditions.other: prebiotic pools, slightly acidic to neutral pH, extended time or thermal cycling

reactions.mechanism: Water attacks the amide carbonyl carbon of glycinamide, forming a tetrahedral intermediate that collapses to release NH₃ and glycine (H₂NCH₂COOH). The reaction is thermodynamically favorable but kinetically slow at neutral pH, requiring elevated temperatures or long residence times. Thermal cycling in geothermal pools accelerates the reaction. The released NH₃ is recycled into upstream reactions.
reactions.reasoning: Amide hydrolysis to carboxylic acids is a fundamental reaction in prebiotic amino acid synthesis. Danger et al. (2012) showed complete conversion of glycinamide to glycine under hydrothermal cycling conditions. This step is the final conversion in the Strecker pathway and has been demonstrated under various plausible prebiotic conditions (Bada, 2013). The reaction half-life at 25°C and neutral pH is ~100 years but drops to hours at 100°C, consistent with geothermal pool cycling.

reactions.name: Formose-type aldol self-condensation of formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: slightly alkaline pH 9-10, Ca(OH)₂ or borate present, concentrated pools

reactions.mechanism: Two molecules of formaldehyde undergo base-catalyzed aldol condensation. The first HCHO is deprotonated at the slightly alkaline surface to generate a carbanion (or enolate equivalent), which attacks the carbonyl of a second HCHO molecule. Montmorillonite interlayer spaces concentrate formaldehyde and provide the alkaline microenvironment. Borate minerals complex with the diol product (glycolaldehyde), shifting the equilibrium toward product formation and preventing further uncontrolled polymerization (formose runaway).
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction, first reported by Butlerow (1861), produces glycolaldehyde as the initial C₂ product from formaldehyde. Breslow (1959) elucidated the autocatalytic mechanism. Ricardo et al. (2004) showed that borate minerals stabilize sugar intermediates and prevent runaway polymerization. Montmorillonite catalysis of aldol condensations has been demonstrated by Cody et al. (2004). Glycolaldehyde has been detected in interstellar medium (Jørgensen et al., 2012), supporting its prebiotic relevance. This step diverts formaldehyde from the Strecker branch toward the glyoxylic acid branch.

reactions.name: UV photooxidation of glycolaldehyde to glyoxylic acid

conditions.other: UV irradiation (λ 200-300 nm), shallow oxidizing surface pools, Fe³⁺ may serve as secondary photo-oxidant

reactions.mechanism: UV photons generate reactive oxygen species (hydroxyl radicals OH•, superoxide O₂⁻•) on TiO₂ surfaces via electron-hole separation. These ROS selectively oxidize the primary alcohol group of glycolaldehyde (HOCH₂CHO) to an aldehyde, yielding glyoxal (OHCCHO), which is further oxidized by water-derived OH• to glyoxylic acid (OHCCOOH). The aldehyde group is more resistant to oxidation than the alcohol under controlled UV dose. Fe³⁺ can also serve as a photo-Fenton oxidant in the absence of O₂.
reactions.reasoning: TiO₂ photocatalytic oxidation of organic molecules is well-established and prebiotically relevant given the abundance of TiO₂ minerals (rutile, anatase) on early Earth. Zhou & Guzman (2014) demonstrated UV-driven oxidation of glycolaldehyde in aqueous solutions. Rapf & Vaida (2016) reviewed aqueous photochemistry of small organic molecules relevant to prebiotic chemistry. The Fe³⁺ photo-Fenton pathway provides an alternative oxidant pathway under anoxic conditions, as demonstrated by Braterman et al. (1983). This converts glycolaldehyde (from the formose branch) into glyoxylic acid, the key substrate for reductive amination to glycine.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of glyoxylic acid to glycine

conditions.other: prebiotic pools with dissolved Fe²⁺ as stoichiometric reductant, pH 6-8, thermal cycling

reactions.mechanism: Step 1: Glyoxylic acid (OHCCOOH) condenses with NH₃ via nucleophilic addition to the aldehyde, forming an α-amino-α-hydroxy acid intermediate that dehydrates to an imine (glyoxylic acid oxime: HN=CHCOOH). Step 2: Fe²⁺ donates two electrons to reduce the C=N imine bond, yielding glycine (H₂NCH₂COOH) and Fe³⁺. The Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ transition provides the thermodynamic driving force for the overall reductive amination. This is analogous to biological glutamate dehydrogenase chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of α-keto acids by NH₃ in the presence of reducing agents is a well-established prebiotic amino acid synthesis route. Kitadai et al. (2018) demonstrated reductive amination of glyoxylic acid to glycine using Fe²⁺ and other reductants under simulated prebiotic conditions. Wang et al. (2005) showed that α-keto acids undergo reductive amination in the presence of NH₃ and iron minerals. Fe²⁺ is the explicit stoichiometric reductant, addressing the requirement for an identified electron source. This route is independent of and complementary to the Strecker pathway.

reactions.name: Carbonylation-amination of formaldehyde to glycine on FeNiS surfaces

conditions.other: CO partial pressure ~1-5 bar, NH₃ from hydrothermal fluid, alkaline vent conditions

reactions.mechanism: On FeNiS mineral surfaces, CO inserts into the Fe-CH₂NH₂ bond formed from formaldehyde and ammonia, generating an N-bound aminoacetyl-metal intermediate. Hydrolytic release from the surface yields glycine directly. This is a surface organometallic mechanism analogous to CO insertion in homogeneous catalysis. The key step is the C-C bond formation via CO insertion into the metal-alkyl bond. Iron sulfide cluster surfaces provide the necessary coordination environment.
reactions.reasoning: Huber & Wächtershäuser (1997, 2006) demonstrated that α-amino acids including glycine can be formed from CO, NH₃, and aldehydes on FeS/NiS (iron-nickel sulfide) surfaces at 100°C under hydrothermal conditions via a carbonylation-amination mechanism analogous to the Monsanto acetic acid process. This 'iron-sulfur world' pathway represents a direct one-pot hydrothermal route to glycine, bypassing the need for surface chemistry. Cody et al. (2000) showed similar carbon fixation chemistry on FeS surfaces at elevated temperatures. This pathway is thermodynamically driven by the high energy of CO and requires no UV or wet-dry cycling.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formate → formaldehyde → Strecker glycine (via greigite CO₂ reduction)
pathways.description: CO₂ is reduced to formate on greigite at alkaline vents, formate is further reduced to formaldehyde on native iron, formaldehyde undergoes Strecker synthesis with NH₃ and HCN in surface pools on montmorillonite, and aminoacetonitrile is hydrolyzed through glycinamide to glycine in prebiotic pools.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal RWGS → FTT formaldehyde → Strecker glycine
pathways.description: CO₂ is converted to CO via reverse water-gas shift on magnetite at high-temperature vents, CO is partially hydrogenated to formaldehyde via Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry on magnetite, then formaldehyde enters the Strecker pathway at the surface to produce glycine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal formate → formaldehyde → formose-glyoxylate → glycine
pathways.description: CO₂ is reduced through formate to formaldehyde at vents, formaldehyde self-condenses to glycolaldehyde via formose chemistry in surface pools with borate stabilization, glycolaldehyde is photooxidized to glyoxylic acid by UV/TiO₂, and glyoxylic acid undergoes reductive amination with NH₃ and Fe²⁺ to yield glycine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal RWGS → FTT formaldehyde → formose-glyoxylate → glycine
pathways.description: CO₂ is converted to CO and then formaldehyde via RWGS and FTT on magnetite, formaldehyde enters the formose-glyoxylate-reductive amination pathway to glycine.

pathways.name: Direct hydrothermal carbonylation-amination via formate-derived HCHO
pathways.description: CO₂ is reduced to formate then formaldehyde at alkaline vents. Simultaneously, CO₂ is converted to CO via RWGS. Formaldehyde, CO, and NH₃ react directly on FeNiS mineral surfaces to produce glycine in a single-pot hydrothermal synthesis without requiring surface UV chemistry.

pathways.name: Direct hydrothermal carbonylation-amination via FTT-derived HCHO
pathways.description: CO₂ is converted to CO via RWGS on magnetite, CO is partially hydrogenated to formaldehyde via FTT. The CO and formaldehyde react with NH₃ on iron-sulfide surfaces to directly produce glycine via carbonylation-amination in a purely hydrothermal pathway.

pathways.name: HCN-formamide → NH₃ supplemented Strecker pathway
pathways.description: HCN is photocatalytically hydrated to formamide in surface pools (TiO₂), formamide is hydrolyzed on montmorillonite to yield formate and NH₃. The NH₃ produced feeds directly into the Strecker synthesis with formaldehyde (from hydrothermal sources) and HCN, producing aminoacetonitrile → glycinamide → glycine. This pathway provides a surface-indigenous source of reactive NH₃.

pathways.name: HCN-formamide → NH₃ supplemented reductive amination pathway
pathways.description: HCN is photocatalytically hydrated to formamide, which is hydrolyzed to formate and NH₃ on montmorillonite. The NH₃ feeds into the reductive amination of glyoxylic acid (produced via the formose-photooxidation branch) with Fe²⁺ as reductant, yielding glycine. This couples the HCN-derived nitrogen supply with the formose-derived carbon skeleton.

pathways.name: Direct surface Strecker synthesis from delivered small molecules
pathways.description: Formaldehyde (delivered from hydrothermal vents or atmospheric photochemistry), NH₃, and HCN (from atmospheric sources) react directly in surface pools via the Strecker mechanism on montmorillonite to produce aminoacetonitrile, which is hydrolyzed stepwise through glycinamide to glycine. This is the shortest and most experimentally validated pathway.

pathways.name: Direct surface formose-glyoxylate reductive amination from delivered HCHO
pathways.description: Formaldehyde (from hydrothermal sources) undergoes aldol self-condensation to glycolaldehyde in surface pools with borate/clay catalysis, glycolaldehyde is UV-photooxidized to glyoxylic acid on TiO₂, and glyoxylic acid undergoes reductive amination with NH₃ and Fe²⁺ in prebiotic pools. This provides a Strecker-independent route to glycine from formaldehyde.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong Strecker pathway with full Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical progression. The weakest step is rxn_003 (formate→formaldehyde on native iron), which is thermodynamically uphill and has limited experimental support. The Strecker core (rxn_007–009) is very well established prebiotically. Greigite-catalysed CO₂ reduction is well supported by Roldan et al. and Preiner et al.
suggestions: Consider whether awaruite (Ni₃Fe) or other alloy catalysts might improve rxn_003 feasibility
Specify pH and temperature conditions for formate reduction

pathway_evaluations.feedback: RWGS followed by FTT formaldehyde synthesis feeds into the Strecker pathway. rxn_004 is the bottleneck: formaldehyde is a transient FTT intermediate that is typically further reduced to methanol on magnetite surfaces. Trapping formaldehyde at useful concentrations is challenging but not implausible at rapid quenching/dilution near vent mixing zones.
suggestions: Address formaldehyde stability and quenching mechanisms in FTT synthesis

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The formose-glyoxylate branch is creative but has two moderate-feasibility steps. rxn_010 (formose self-condensation to glycolaldehyde) typically requires alkaline conditions and a catalyst like Ca(OH)₂ or borate minerals – the network should specify this. rxn_011 (UV photooxidation selectivity) may yield mixed products. rxn_003 remains the upstream bottleneck.
suggestions: Specify borate or Ca(OH)₂ mineral catalyst for rxn_010
Address selectivity of UV photooxidation in rxn_011
Consider direct glyoxylate production from HCN/formaldehyde as alternative

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Similar to P3 but uses RWGS-FTT front-end. Accumulates moderate-feasibility steps (rxn_004, rxn_010, rxn_011) making the overall pathway less reliable than the Strecker branches.

Entirely hydrothermal pathway (no Surface or Biochemical stages). Inspired by Huber & Wächtershäuser carbonylation chemistry. Lacks the environmental progression expected in the network design. rxn_013 combines HCHO + CO + NH₃ in a single step on FeNiS, which has some experimental support but the specific combination to produce glycine directly is less well-documented than the Strecker or reductive amination routes. rxn_003 remains a bottleneck.
suggestions: Strengthen literature support for rxn_013 mechanism
Consider whether glycine produced hydrothermally could still feed into biochemical concentration/polymerization steps

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Shortest pathway (3 steps), entirely hydrothermal. Elegantly simple but lacks environmental progression. CO is produced alongside HCHO via FTT, and both are co-consumed in rxn_013, which is mechanistically plausible but difficult to validate. The lack of Surface/Biochemical stages weakens environmental scoring.
suggestions: Add a downstream surface or biochemical concentration step to improve environmental flow

pathway_evaluations.feedback: HCN-formamide hydrolysis supplies NH₃ and formate to the Strecker branch. Chemically sound individual steps; formamide hydrolysis on clay is well-supported (Saladino et al.). However, this pathway starts at Surface and misses Hydrothermal front-end generation of HCHO, which must be supplied from another pathway. The NH₃-supplementation role is valuable for the network but the pathway alone is incomplete without HCHO source.
suggestions: Clarify that HCHO for rxn_007 must be imported from hydrothermal branches
Could specify TiO₂ or ZnS mineral photocatalyst for rxn_005

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Supplies NH₃ from formamide hydrolysis to the reductive amination branch. Coherence is weaker because glyoxylic acid (mol_013) must be sourced from the formose-photooxidation branch (P3/P4), and Fe²⁺ source is assumed. The pathway itself is more of a supplementary feeder than a standalone route.
suggestions: Explicitly connect to glyoxylic acid source pathways

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The chemically strongest pathway – the classical Strecker synthesis is the single most-validated prebiotic glycine route (Miller 1957, Peltzer & Bada 1978). Only Surface→Surface→Biochemical environmental progression; no hydrothermal front-end. Assumes all starting materials (HCHO, NH₃, HCN) are delivered exogenously or from other branches.
suggestions: Could add mineral catalyst specification for Strecker step (e.g., montmorillonite)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Short surface-to-biochemical pathway via formose and photooxidation. Chemical feasibility is limited by selectivity issues in both rxn_010 and rxn_011. Formose reaction typically produces a complex mixture; glycolaldehyde selectivity requires borate stabilization. Photooxidation selectivity for glyoxylic acid over other products is uncertain.
suggestions: Add borate minerals to rxn_010 for glycolaldehyde selectivity
Provide evidence for photooxidation selectivity in rxn_011

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-designed, convergent prebiotic synthesis network for glycine with strong topology and multiple redundant pathways. Key strengths: (1) Formaldehyde serves as an excellent hub with degree 5, connecting hydrothermal carbon fixation to both Strecker and glyoxylate branches. (2) The Strecker pathway core (rxn_007→008→009) is extremely well-established prebiotically. (3) Multiple independent hydrothermal front-ends (greigite CO₂ reduction, RWGS, FTT) provide robustness. (4) Environmental flow is well-maintained with clear Hydrothermal→Surface→Biochemical progression in the main pathways. Key weaknesses: (1) Two of four declared hub molecules (aminoacetonitrile, glyoxylic acid) have degree 2 and do not truly function as hubs. (2) The formate-to-formaldehyde reduction (rxn_003) and FTT formaldehyde capture (rxn_004) are the principal thermodynamic bottlenecks. (3) The formose-photooxidation branch (rxn_010→011) has selectivity concerns that are not adequately addressed. (4) Mineral catalysts are not specified for all surface reactions (rxn_010 lacks mineral specification). (5) Literature citations are implied but not explicitly provided in reaction descriptions. Despite these issues, the network is chemically plausible, topologically sound, and successfully generates glycine through multiple convergent routes. The Strecker branch (P1, P2, P9) is the strongest component of the network.
